About Me: I’m Aswin Vishwaa — a Full-Stack and AI Developer who didn’t start out aiming for AI or big dreams. I just wanted a job. I began with C, Python, and Java, solving basic problems without even knowing what they were building toward. Before any code, I spent my first year as an active NCC Air Wing cadet, attending camps, talking with strangers, leading groups. That year didn’t make me a coder — it made me a communicator, a leader, and someone who can stand on a stage without fear.

My curiosity drives me. I love learning new things just to know how they work. That mindset led me to build my first real project — a movie recommendation system from a YouTube tutorial. That one project flipped a switch. I dove straight into hands-on learning, skipping the textbooks and diving into tools. But my real evolution happened during the Glaucoma Screening App project — an AI-based diagnostic tool built for the Smart India Hackathon. That’s where I learned the true grind: managing GPU memory, handling batch sizes, understanding the cost of bad data, and wrestling with model deployment. That project made me a developer — not just a student.

I'm obsessed with building things that work — fast. Not because it’s a flex, but because I’m a student. I don’t have money to spend on fancy servers or paid APIs. I push the free tier to its absolute edge. I use Groq, Twilio, and every open tool I can find — because I have to, and because it's a challenge I enjoy. My style? Speed over polish, proof-of-concept over perfection. If it works, it ships. I'll clean it later.

I work solo — not by choice, but by nature. I haven’t done big team projects yet, but I adapt fast. My workflow is brutally direct: read the problem, research the end-to-end plan, build in sections, test as I go. I don't write clean code at first. I write working code. Once I see it’s possible, I optimize. Chaos first, clarity later.

One recent example? I got two days to build a full-stack AI intern project. Never touched Docker, LangChain, or PostgreSQL before. I had no time to install PostgreSQL locally, so I threw it in Docker — all I knew was "Docker is like a tiffin box for your app." That was enough. I used what I learned on the fly, brainstormed walking around alone, and built something functional from scratch.

Why do I keep building? Curiosity. Hunger. I don’t want my brain to rust. I make projects to make my life easier, automate the boring stuff, and prove that yes — it can be done. If something takes 30 minutes a day, I’ll spend a weekend building a tool to save that time forever. Sometimes my laziness becomes innovation.

I’m not just learning AI — I’m making it work, even when the odds and time aren’t on my side.

Project: Glaucoma Screening Web Application

G-Vision is an AI-powered web application built to screen for early signs of glaucoma using fundus images of the eyes. It was developed as part of a college team submission for the Smart India Hackathon (SIH), where I personally led and built the technical backbone — from dataset preparation to model training and web integration.

The project addresses a real-world crisis: in India, over 90% of glaucoma cases remain undiagnosed due to a lack of affordable, accessible screening tools. G-Vision provides a portable, AI-driven solution capable of performing early detection without needing a medical specialist.

The core of the system is a custom-built U-Net model designed for medical image segmentation. After struggling to integrate initial object detection models like YOLOv9 and Faster R-CNN, I pivoted toward U-Net based on insights from research papers. I trained it from scratch using annotated fundus image datasets (e.g., Drishti-GS, REFUGE), and tuned it specifically to extract the optic cup and disc. The vertical cup-to-disc ratio (VcDR), a key biomarker for glaucoma, is calculated from these segmented regions.

Key Features:
- Dual image upload (left and right eyes)
- U-Net based segmentation of optic cup & disc
- VcDR calculation & diagnostic outcome (Negative/Positive)
- Ground-truth validated results
- Responsive web interface with original vs mask visualization
- Slider-based comparison modal for precise image overlay

Challenges included data quality, medical annotation alignment, and integrating a Python-based ML pipeline into a clean Flask web app. We also had to optimize preprocessing (resizing, normalization) and manage memory constraints during model training.

Although the hardware device is still under development, the software is fully functional and tested. It’s not deployed publicly yet and remains in a private repository. If given more time or resources, I would retrain the model for even better clinical accuracy and build out a cloud inference pipeline.

This project changed how I think about AI. It wasn’t just a model — it was a product aimed at saving vision. The lessons I learned here in deployment, model selection, and domain-specific ML made this my turning point as an engineer.

Project: Smart Email Assistant – LLM-Based WhatsApp Email Notifier

This project was born from two things: my curiosity to explore large language models (LLMs), and my personal frustration with constantly checking emails. I wanted to build something that could fetch my important emails, summarize them using AI, and deliver those summaries straight to my WhatsApp — where I actually pay attention. That’s how the Smart Email Assistant was born.

The system integrates Gmail, Groq's LLaMA 3, and Twilio to form a fully functional assistant that can:
- Automatically fetch recent emails from Gmail
- Use LLMs to filter and summarize important messages
- Deliver summaries to WhatsApp via Twilio
- Let the user reply with simple commands like '1' or '1R' to read summaries or generate professional responses

This wasn’t just an experiment — it became part of my workflow.

🛠️ Technology Stack:
- Groq API (LLaMA 3): Summarizes emails and generates tone-matching replies
- Gmail API: Fetches recent unread emails using OAuth 2.0
- Twilio API: Sends WhatsApp messages and receives replies
- Flask: Backend server that handles webhook events
- ngrok: Tunnels localhost to the public web for Twilio integration
- JSON context store: Maintains user state and email index

💡 How it works:
1. `gmail_fetcher.py` connects to Gmail and fetches the latest emails.
2. Each email is passed through `llm_processor.py`, which sends it to Groq’s LLM for summarization.
3. `whatsapp_bot.py` formats and sends a clean WhatsApp message with numbered emails.
4. If the user replies with `1`, `2`, etc., the webhook in `webhook_handler.py` serves the right summary.
5. If they reply `1R`, `2R`, etc., the LLM generates a professional reply which is sent back instantly.

✅ Reply Commands:
- `1`: View summary of Email #1
- `1R`: Generate and send a professional reply for Email #1

📁 Folder Architecture Highlights:
- `main.py`: Entry point for fetching & sending
- `webhook_handler.py`: Listens to WhatsApp replies
- `llm_processor.py`: Handles summarization & reply generation
- `context_store.py`: Manages context via JSON

🔐 The `.env` file securely stores all API keys and credentials, including Groq, Gmail, and Twilio auth tokens.

This project taught me how to integrate LLMs into real-world automation tasks, how to maintain conversational state over a stateless API like WhatsApp, and how to build truly useful tools for daily productivity. It also showed me how powerful LLMs can be when paired with thoughtful backend workflows. What started as a lazy solution became one of my most practically useful AI tools to date.

Project: Doctor-Agent – LLM-Powered Appointment and Reporting System

Doctor-Agent is a production-ready, full-stack AI assistant that allows patients to book doctor appointments through natural language, and enables doctors to retrieve appointment and symptom-based analytics in real time. This solo project was built as part of an interview assignment during my internship selection process, giving me only a couple of days to ideate, research, and deliver a functional end-to-end system.

The assistant combines LangChain, Groq LLaMA 3, FastAPI, React, Docker, PostgreSQL, and Google Calendar into a cohesive agentic system using the MCP (Model Context Protocol) architecture. Patients can book appointments with free-text prompts like 'I need to see Dr. Mehta tomorrow at 10 AM', while doctors can ask things like 'How many patients had fever this week?' and receive structured responses.

This project is a prime example of how I dive straight into hands-on problem-solving before touching theory. I had zero prior experience with Docker, PostgreSQL, or LangChain, but I integrated all three under pressure by learning on the fly. To save setup time, I used Docker as a containerized environment — treating it like a “tiffin box” that held the database and backend services neatly.

🧠 Key Features:
- Natural language appointment booking
- Doctor analytics (appointments, symptom-based queries)
- Google Calendar event integration
- Email confirmation system via SMTP
- MCP-based LLM tool registration and invocation
- Dockerized backend, DB seeding, and modular architecture
- Session memory preservation with LangChain's ConversationBufferMemory

🛠️ Stack Overview:
- Frontend: React + Vite + Tailwind CSS
- Backend: FastAPI + Docker + LangChain
- LLM: Groq API (LLaMA 3)
- DB: PostgreSQL (with Docker-based seeding)
- External: Gmail SMTP, Google Calendar API

🗂️ Folder Structure:
- `/backend/`: FastAPI + MCP tool registry
- `/frontend/`: React chat UI
- `docker-compose.yml`: Orchestrates backend, DB, and seeding

📈 Agent Flow:
1. Patient/Doctor enters natural prompt
2. LLM selects the right MCP tool from registry
3. Tool performs action (e.g., DB query, appointment booking)
4. Result returned as human-readable response

This project proved I can break into new technologies under pressure and deliver robust, scalable results. It reflects my engineering style: speed-first execution, curiosity-driven learning, and functional integration across the full stack. Whether it's backend orchestration, agent memory handling, or working with real-time APIs — Doctor-Agent was built to work like a production tool, not a demo.

Project: Movie Recommendation System

This was my very first project in machine learning, built as a foundation to understand how recommender systems work. It includes two separate models: one using a static dataset, and another that fetches real-time data from an external API. Although it has no frontend or UI, this project laid the groundwork for how I understand similarity metrics, content-based filtering, and API integration.

🧠 Model 1: Static Dataset Recommender
- Uses a local Excel (.xlsx) file as the data source
- Recommends movies based on genres and ratings using cosine similarity
- Basic pandas and scikit-learn implementation

🌐 Model 2: API-Based Recommender
- Fetches live movie data from an external API (like TMDB)
- Builds a real-time similarity matrix for recommendations
- Introduced me to API request handling, parsing responses, and fallback logic

This project may look simple on the surface, but it helped me grasp how raw data can be processed and converted into intelligent outputs — something I now apply in more advanced systems. It was also my first step toward building AI tools that interact with real-world data.

Project: Developer Portfolio with RAG-Powered AI Chatbot

This is my personal developer portfolio, designed to be more than just a static webpage. It's an intelligent, interactive representation of my work — featuring a custom-built Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) chatbot that can answer questions about my experience, projects, and skills.

🛠️ Tech Stack:
- Frontend: React.js (Vite)
- Styling: Tailwind CSS
- UI: ShadCN UI, Lucide Icons
- Animations: Framer Motion
- Routing: React Router
- Hosting: GitHub Pages / Vercel

🤖 AI Chatbot Architecture:
- Embeddings: Local generation using @xenova/transformers (MiniLM)
- Retrieval: Cosine similarity on a JSON-based context file (docs.json)
- Generation: Groq API (LLaMA 3)
- Zero backend: Entire chatbot works client-side on the browser

📦 Portfolio Sections:
- Animated landing page with CTA buttons (Projects, Resume, Contact)
- About section with bio, skills, and language list
- Projects section with detailed cards, GitHub links, and filters (AI/Web)
- Resume download & contact section
- Embedded chatbot trained on personal data, works offline with local embeddings

📈 Performance & Privacy:
- Vite for fast builds and lazy loading
- Tailwind CSS for responsive design
- No trackers or analytics unless added manually
- No backend: Embeddings are computed client-side

This project reflects my philosophy: show, don’t just tell. Rather than listing my skills, I built a system where people can ask about my work directly — and the chatbot responds intelligently using real context. It’s not just a portfolio; it’s a demonstration of what I build, how I think, and how I scale.

Project: AI Brain Tumor Radiotherapy Planning Pipeline (Freelance, Oct 2025 – Present)

Built a fully automated end-to-end AI pipeline for brain tumour radiotherapy planning on BraTS2020 dataset. One of the first systems to integrate deep learning segmentation, medical VLM reporting, RAG clinical guidance, and automated RT contouring in a single unified framework — running on dual Tesla T4 GPUs (29.2GB total VRAM).

Four-stage architecture:
1. 3D UNet — segments whole tumour (WT), tumour core (TC), and enhancing tumour (ET) from multi-modal MRI (T1, T1CE, T2, FLAIR). Achieved Dice WT=0.9288, TC=0.8748, ET=0.8876.
2. MedGemma 1.5 — Google's medical VLM that generates structured clinical radiology reports from segmentation outputs and T1-CE images.
3. RAG Clinical Guidance Agent — Llama-3 + FAISS hybrid retrieval (FAISS dense + BM25 sparse + cross-encoder reranker) grounded in ESTRO-EANO 2023 published guidelines (370 pages).
4. GTV/CTV/PTV Contouring — GPU-accelerated 3D morphological operations implementing ESTRO-EANO 2023 (15mm CTV, 60Gy/30fx) and RTOG dual-phase protocols. 50× faster than CPU scipy baseline via torch max_pool3d.

Tech stack: PyTorch 2.5.1, MONAI 1.4.0, MedGemma 1.5 (4B-IT), Llama-3 (4-bit QLoRA), LangChain, FAISS-GPU, BGE Embeddings, BM25Retriever, CrossEncoder Reranker, NiBabel, SciPy, scikit-image, OpenCV.

Blog: Portfolio RAG Chatbot Architecture

I built a portfolio-integrated AI chatbot using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). The goal was to create a fully client-side assistant that answers queries about my work, skills, and projects without needing a heavy backend.

Architecture:
- Embeddings: Uses @xenova/transformers with the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model for in-browser text embedding.
- Vector Search: Queries are converted to vectors and compared against pre-embedded documents using cosine similarity.
- Top-k Retrieval: Top 3 most relevant chunks from docs.json are selected.
- LLM Response: These chunks are passed to Groq's LLaMA 3.3 API for generating intelligent answers.

Key Features:
- Runs on Vercel with no heavy backend
- Free-tier optimized: all embedding happens locally, Groq is used only for final response
- Smart enough to answer project-specific questions like 'What is Aswin's experience with LLMs?' or 'Tell me about the Glaucoma project'

Deployment: Hosted on Vercel. Uses docs.json as the single source of knowledge. No server, database, or backend logic needed.
